steps.bodyLatex: The question requires the answer to two significant figures. The first two significant digits in \(50.688\) are \(5\) and \(0\). Since the next digit is \(6\), we round up to get \(51\text{ tonnes}\).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: The estimated area is \(18\text{ m}^2\) and the weight of the concrete is \(51\text{ tonnes}\) (to two significant figures).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to divide the total width by the number of strips to find \(h\).
Rounding the intermediate volume or weight too early, which can lead to an incorrect final answer.
Confusing decimal places with significant figures when rounding the final result.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check that the area is reasonable by comparing it to a rectangle of average height: \(8.0 \times 2.1 \approx 16.8\text{ m}^2\), which is close to our calculated \(18\text{ m}^2\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses future value annuity tables to calculate either the required periodic contribution or the final accumulated balance.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the number of periods \(n\) and the interest rate per period \(r\). Use the provided table to find the Future Value Interest Factor (FVIF) and multiply or divide the principal amount accordingly.

steps.title: Part (a): Finding the annual contribution
steps.bodyLatex: To find the annual contribution, we use the formula \(A = P \times \text{FVIF}\), where \(A\) is the target amount and \(P\) is the contribution. Rearranging gives \(P = \frac{A}{\text{FVIF}}\). For 10 years at 6% per annum, the table provides a factor of \(13.181\). Thus, \(P = \frac{450000}{13.181} \approx 34140.05\).

steps.title: Part (a): Final rounding
steps.bodyLatex: The question asks for the answer to the nearest dollar. Rounding \(34140.05\) to the nearest whole number gives an annual contribution of \(\$34\,140\).

steps.title: Part (b): Adjusting for quarterly compounding
steps.bodyLatex: Since the contributions are quarterly, we adjust the rate and periods. The quarterly interest rate is \(r = \frac{6\%}{4} = 1.5\%\). The total number of periods over 10 years is \(n = 10 \times 4 = 40\). From the table, the FVIF for \(n=40\) at \(1.5\%\) is \(54.268\).

steps.title: Part (b): Calculating the final balance
steps.bodyLatex: Using the formula \(A = P \times \text{FVIF}\) with the quarterly contribution \(P = \$8535\), we calculate the total future value: \(A = 8535 \times 54.268 = 463177.38\). Micky will have \(\$463\,177.38\) at the end of 10 years.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to adjust the interest rate and number of periods for quarterly compounding in part (b).
Using the wrong column or row in the interest factor table.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: In part (a), check that \(34140 \times 13.181 \approx 450000\). In part (b), ensure the total number of periods \(n\) matches the frequency of the contributions.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves calculating the area of a composite shape and applying ratio calculations to determine the quantity of materials required.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), subtract the area of the inner garden from the total area of the rectangle. For (b), use the ratio parts to find the mass of cement, then divide by the weight per bag.

steps.title: Calculate the area of the path
steps.bodyLatex: The total area is defined by the outer dimensions \(8\text{ m} \times 3\text{ m} = 24\text{ m}^2\). The inner garden dimensions are reduced by the \(0.5\text{ m}\) path width on the sides and bottom. The inner width is \(8 - 0.5 - 0.5 = 7\text{ m}\) and the inner height is \(3 - 0.5 = 2.5\text{ m}\). The inner area is \(7\text{ m} \times 2.5\text{ m} = 17.5\text{ m}^2\). The path area is \(24 - 17.5 = 6.5\text{ m}^2\).

steps.title: Determine the proportion of cement
steps.bodyLatex: The ratio of crushed rock to sand to cement is \(4:2:1\). The total number of parts is \(4 + 2 + 1 = 7\). Therefore, cement makes up \(\frac{1}{7}\) of the total weight of the concrete mix.

steps.title: Calculate the mass of cement required
steps.bodyLatex: Kim needs \(2.1\text{ tonnes}\) of mix. The mass of cement required is \(\frac{1}{7} \times 2.1\text{ tonnes} = 0.3\text{ tonnes}\). Since the bags are measured in kilograms, convert this to kilograms: \(0.3 \times 1000 = 300\text{ kg}\).

steps.title: Calculate the number of bags
steps.bodyLatex: Each bag contains \(15\text{ kg}\) of cement. To find the number of bags needed, divide the total mass of cement by the mass per bag: \(\frac{300\text{ kg}}{15\text{ kg/bag}} = 20\text{ bags}\).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) The area of the path is \(6.5\text{ m}^2\). (b) Kim needs to buy \(20\) bags of cement.
commonMistakesLatex: In part (a), forgetting to subtract the path width from both sides of the rectangle (e.g., subtracting 0.5 instead of 1.0).
In part (b), failing to convert tonnes to kilograms before dividing by the bag weight.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (b), check the total weight: \(4\text{ parts} + 2\text{ parts} + 1\text{ part} = 7\text{ parts}\). If \(1\text{ part} = 0.3\text{ tonnes}\), then \(7 \times 0.3 = 2.1\text{ tonnes}\), which matches the total.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses trigonometry in right-angled triangles to solve for distances and bearings based on geometric relationships.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, identify the right-angled triangles formed by the positions. Since \(P\) is due south of \(X\) and \(C\) is due west of \(Y\), we can use basic trigonometric ratios like \(\cos \theta\) and \(\sin \theta\) to find the missing lengths and angles.

steps.title: Find the distance XP
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(Y\) is on a bearing of \(120^{\circ}\) from \(X\), the angle between the North line and the line \(XY\) is \(120^{\circ}\). Because \(P\) is due south of \(X\), the angle \(\angle PXY\) is \(180^{\circ} - 120^{\circ} = 60^{\circ}\). In the right-angled triangle \( riangle PXY\), we have \(\cos(60^{\circ}) = \frac{XP}{15}\). Thus, \(XP = 15 \times \cos(60^{\circ}) = 7.5 \text{ km}\).

steps.title: Identify the triangle CXP
steps.bodyLatex: We now consider the right-angled triangle \( riangle CXP\). We know the hypotenuse \(XC = 40 \text{ km}\) and the adjacent side \(XP = 7.5 \text{ km}\). Let \(\theta\) be the angle \(\angle CXP\). Using the cosine ratio, \(\cos(\theta) = \frac{7.5}{40} = 0.1875\).

steps.title: Calculate the angle CXP
steps.bodyLatex: To find \(\theta\), calculate the inverse cosine: \(\theta = \cos^{-1}(0.1875) \approx 79.20^{\circ}\). In degrees and minutes, this is approximately \(79^{\circ} 12'\).

steps.title: Determine the bearing of C from X
steps.bodyLatex: The bearing is measured clockwise from North. Since \(P\) is due south of \(X\) (bearing \(180^{\circ}\)) and \(C\) is to the west of the line \(XP\), we subtract \(\theta\) from \(180^{\circ}\) to find the bearing: \(180^{\circ} + 79.20^{\circ} = 259.20^{\circ}\). Rounding to the nearest degree, we get \(259^{\circ}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the bearing angle with the interior angle of the triangle.
Forgetting that the bearing is measured clockwise from North, leading to an incorrect addition or subtraction of the calculated angle.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check that \(\sin(60^{\circ}) = \frac{PY}{15}\), so \(PY \approx 12.99 \text{ km}\). In \( riangle CXP\), \(CP = \sqrt{40^2 - 7.5^2} \approx 39.29 \text{ km}\). These values are consistent with the geometry.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem requires comparing two depreciation models over a 3-year period to determine which results in a lower salvage value.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the formula for each depreciation method. The straight-line method uses a constant annual deduction, while the declining-balance method uses a percentage reduction applied to the remaining value each year.

steps.title: Straight-line method
steps.bodyLatex: The straight-line method subtracts a fixed amount each year. With an initial value \(V_0 = \$60\,000\) and an annual depreciation \(D = \$3500\), the value after \(n=3\) years is calculated as \(S = V_0 - (D \times n)\). Substituting the values gives \(S = 60\,000 - (3500 \times 3) = 60\,000 - 10\,500 = \$49\,500\).

steps.title: Declining-balance method
steps.bodyLatex: The declining-balance method uses the formula \(S = V_0(1 - r)^n\), where \(r\) is the annual rate of depreciation. Here, \(r = 12\% = 0.12\) and \(n = 3\). Substituting these values gives \(S = 60\,000(1 - 0.12)^3 = 60\,000(0.88)^3\).

steps.title: Calculate declining-balance value
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate the power first: \(0.88^3 = 0.681472\). Then, multiply by the initial value: \(S = 60\,000 \times 0.681472 = \$40\,888.32\).

steps.title: Comparison and conclusion
steps.bodyLatex: Compare the two results: the straight-line method yields \(\$49\,500\), while the declining-balance method yields \(\$40\,888.32\). Since \(\$40\,888.32 < \$49\,500\), the declining-balance method results in a lower salvage value.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: The declining-balance method results in a lower salvage value of \(\$40\,888.32\).
commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the two formulas or applying the percentage rate incorrectly by multiplying by 3 instead of using the power of 3.
Rounding the declining-balance result too early, which can lead to an inaccurate final comparison.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check that \(0.88^3\) is less than 1, which confirms the value decreases over time, and ensure the units are in dollars.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves using a loan repayment table to calculate total repayments and the principal amount borrowed.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the correct factor from the table based on the interest rate and loan term. For part (a), multiply the number of thousands borrowed by the factor and the total number of months. For part (b), divide the monthly payment by the factor and multiply by 1000 to find the principal.

steps.title: Part (a): Calculate monthly repayment
steps.bodyLatex: First, determine how many units of \(\$1000\) are in the \(\$520\,000\) loan: \(\frac{520\,000}{1000} = 520\). Using the table for 8% interest over 25 years, the factor is \(7.72\). The monthly repayment is \(520 \times 7.72 = \$4014.40\).

steps.title: Part (a): Calculate total repayment
steps.bodyLatex: The loan term is 25 years, which equals \(25 \times 12 = 300\) months. The total amount repaid is the monthly repayment multiplied by the total number of months: \(\$4014.40 \times 300 = \$1\,204\,320\).

steps.title: Part (b): Identify the factor
steps.bodyLatex: For a loan at 7% per annum over 15 years, locate the corresponding factor in the table. The table indicates the factor for these terms is \(8.99\). This means for every \(\$1000\) borrowed, Chris pays \(\$8.99\) per month.

steps.title: Part (b): Calculate the principal
steps.bodyLatex: To find the total amount borrowed, divide the monthly payment by the factor and multiply by 1000: \(\left( \frac{3596}{8.99} \right) \times 1000 = 400 \times 1000 = \$400\,000\).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) The total amount repaid is \(\$1\,204\,320\). (b) The amount borrowed is \(\$400\,000\).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to multiply the monthly repayment by the total number of months (12 months per year) in part (a).
Using the wrong row or column from the table; always double-check the interest rate and term before selecting the factor.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: In part (b), check the result: \(\$400\,000\) is 400 units of \(\$1000\). Multiplying \(400 \times 8.99 = 3596\), which matches the given monthly repayment.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem requires identifying the value of goods subject to GST and subtracting both the GST and the GST-inclusive value from the total receipt amount.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recognise that the total receipt is composed of three parts: the value of non-GST items, the value of GST-exclusive items, and the GST itself. First, calculate the value of the items that were taxed.

steps.title: Calculate the value of GST-taxed items
steps.bodyLatex: Since GST is \(10\%\) of the value of the taxed items, let \(n\) be the value of these items. We have \(0.10 \times n = \$3.86\). Dividing both sides by \(0.10\), we find \(n = \frac{3.86}{0.10} = \$38.60\).

steps.title: Determine the total value of taxed goods
steps.bodyLatex: The total amount paid for the taxed items includes both the original price of the goods and the GST. This is calculated as \(\$38.60 + \$3.86 = \$42.46\).

steps.title: Calculate the value of non-taxed items
steps.bodyLatex: The total receipt amount of \(\$124.87\) is the sum of the non-taxed items and the total taxed amount (including GST). Subtract the taxed total from the receipt total: \(\$124.87 - \$42.46 = \$82.41\).

steps.title: Verification
steps.bodyLatex: Check the components: \(\$82.41\) (non-taxed) + \(\$38.60\) (taxed base) + \(\$3.86\) (GST) = \(\$124.87\). The sum matches the total on the receipt.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: The value of the items which did not have GST charged was \(\$82.41\).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to add the GST amount back to the base taxed amount before subtracting from the total.
Subtracting only the GST (\(\$3.86\)) from the total, which ignores the base price of the taxed items.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ensure the sum of the non-taxed items, the taxed items, and the GST equals the total receipt value.

workedSolutions.reviewNote: The provided answer and working are insufficient to determine the critical paths or the specific task reduction without the visual network diagram. The logic provided in the prompt's answer key is incomplete as it references tasks H and I without context of the full network structure.
workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Critical path analysis involves identifying the longest path through a network and determining which task reduction will shorten the overall project duration.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To find the critical path, calculate the earliest start and finish times for every path through the network. The critical path is the longest path, which determines the minimum time required to complete the project.

steps.title: Identify all paths
steps.bodyLatex: List every possible sequence of tasks from the start node to the end node. Calculate the total duration for each path by summing the time values assigned to each task in that sequence.

steps.title: Determine the critical paths
steps.bodyLatex: The critical path is the path with the maximum total duration. If multiple paths share this same maximum duration, they are all considered critical paths. Identify these by comparing the sums calculated in the previous step.

steps.title: Analyse the reduction
steps.bodyLatex: To reduce the project duration by 3 hours, you must target a task that lies on all critical paths. If you reduce a task that is only on one critical path, the other critical path will still dictate the project's total time.

steps.title: Calculate the new task duration
steps.bodyLatex: Subtract 3 hours from the original duration of the chosen task. Verify that this reduction successfully lowers the total duration of all critical paths to the target of 14 hours.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: The critical paths are identified by the longest duration sequences, and the task reduction must occur on a shared task to effectively shorten the project duration.
commonMistakesLatex: Reducing a task that is not on the critical path, which will have no effect on the total project time.
Failing to identify all critical paths, leading to an incomplete reduction strategy.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ensure that after the reduction, the sum of the tasks on every critical path equals the new target time of 14 hours.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the compound interest formula to calculate interest accrued over a specific number of days.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that the interest is compounded daily. We use the formula \(A = P(1+r)^n\), where \(P\) is the principal, \(r\) is the daily interest rate, and \(n\) is the number of days.

steps.title: Calculate the daily interest rate
steps.bodyLatex: The annual interest rate is \(13.5\%\), which is \(0.135\) as a decimal. Since there are \(365\) days in a year, the daily interest rate \(r\) is \(\frac{0.135}{365} \approx 0.00036986\).

steps.title: Calculate the total amount owing
steps.bodyLatex: Using the compound interest formula \(A = P(1+r)^n\), where \(P = \$450\), \(r = \frac{0.135}{365}\), and \(n = 21\), we calculate: \(A = 450 \left(1 + \frac{0.135}{365}\right)^{21} \approx \$453.5146\).

steps.title: Find the interest charged
steps.bodyLatex: The interest charged is the difference between the total amount repaid and the original purchase price: \(I = A - P = \$453.51 - \$450 = \$3.51\).

steps.title: Calculate the percentage
steps.bodyLatex: To find what percentage the interest is of the total amount repaid, divide the interest by the total amount and multiply by \(100\): \(\frac{3.51}{453.51} \times 100 \approx 0.774\%\). Rounding to two decimal places gives \(0.77\%\).

commonMistakesLatex: Using the simple interest formula \(I = Prn\) instead of the compound interest formula.
Forgetting to convert the annual percentage rate to a decimal or failing to divide by 365 to get the daily rate.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The interest is a small fraction of the principal, which makes sense for a short 21-day period at a moderate annual rate.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves calculating the mean of bivariate data to locate the regression line's centroid and using the line's properties to determine its equation.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, calculate the mean of the independent variable (\(x\)) and dependent variable (\(y\)). Use the fact that the regression line passes through the point \(( ar{x},  ar{y})\) and the given \(y\)-intercept to find the slope.

steps.title: Calculate the means
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate the mean temperature \( ar{x}\) by summing the ten temperatures and dividing by 10: \( ar{x} = \frac{0+0+0+2+5+7+8+9+9+10}{10} = 5\). The mean gas usage \( ar{y}\) is the total usage divided by the number of days: \( ar{y} = \frac{1840}{10} = 184\). The regression line must pass through the point \((5, 184)\).

steps.title: Determine the regression equation
steps.bodyLatex: The regression line is in the form \(y = mx + c\). We are given the \(y\)-intercept \(c = 236\) (when \(x=0\)). Using the point \((5, 184)\), substitute these values into the equation to find the slope \(m\): \(184 = m(5) + 236\). Solving for \(m\): \(5m = 184 - 236\), so \(5m = -52\), which gives \(m = -10.4\).

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.bodyLatex: Substituting the slope and intercept back into the linear equation gives the final model: \(y = 236 - 10.4x\). This equation allows us to predict gas usage \(y\) for any given temperature \(x\) within the range of the data.

steps.title: Evaluate the prediction
steps.bodyLatex: To check the validity of predicting at \(23^{\circ}\text{C}\), substitute \(x=23\) into the equation: \(y = 236 - 10.4(23) = 236 - 239.2 = -3.2\). Since gas usage cannot be negative, this prediction is physically impossible. This is an example of extrapolation, where the model is used outside the range of the original data.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) Plot \((5, 184)\) and \((0, 236)\). (b) \(y = 236 - 10.4x\). (c) The prediction results in a negative value for gas usage, which is physically impossible.
commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the \(x\) and \(y\) variables when calculating the slope.
Attempting to use the slope formula \(m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}\) without correctly identifying the two points \((0, 236)\) and \((5, 184)\).
Forgetting that the regression line must pass through the mean point \(( ar{x}, \bar{y})\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the slope by checking if the point \((5, 184)\) satisfies the equation: \(236 - 10.4(5) = 236 - 52 = 184\). The calculation is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem requires rearranging a given formula to solve for an unknown variable \(H\) and then converting that decimal time into hours and minutes.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the given values: \(BAC = 0.02\), \(N = 3\), and \(M = 75\). Substitute these into the formula and solve the linear equation for \(H\). Finally, convert the decimal portion of \(H\) into minutes.

steps.title: Substitute and rearrange
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the known values into the formula: \(0.02 = \frac{10(3) - 7.5H}{6.8(75)}\). Simplify the denominator: \(6.8 \times 75 = 510\). Now, multiply both sides by \(510\) to get \(0.02 \times 510 = 30 - 7.5H\), which simplifies to \(10.2 = 30 - 7.5H\).

steps.title: Solve for H
steps.bodyLatex: Rearrange the equation to isolate the term with \(H\): \(7.5H = 30 - 10.2\). This gives \(7.5H = 19.8\). Divide by \(7.5\) to find \(H = \frac{19.8}{7.5} = 2.64\) hours.

steps.title: Convert to time
steps.bodyLatex: The value \(H = 2.64\) represents \(2\) full hours and a fraction of an hour. To find the minutes, multiply the decimal part by \(60\): \(0.64 \times 60 = 38.4\). Rounding to the nearest minute, we get \(38\) minutes. Thus, the total duration is \(2\) hours and \(38\) minutes.

steps.title: Calculate the start time
steps.bodyLatex: Subtract the duration of \(2\) hours and \(38\) minutes from the end time of \(9:00\text{ pm}\). Subtracting \(2\) hours gives \(7:00\text{ pm}\). Subtracting a further \(38\) minutes from \(7:00\text{ pm}\) results in \(6:22\text{ pm}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to multiply the decimal part of the hours by 60 when converting to minutes (e.g., thinking 0.64 hours is 64 minutes).
Errors in algebraic rearrangement when dealing with the fraction.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the result by substituting \(H = 2.64\) back into the original formula: \(\frac{10(3) - 7.5(2.64)}{6.8(75)} = \frac{30 - 19.8}{510} = \frac{10.2}{510} = 0.02\). The calculation is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the standard normal distribution formula to convert a raw score into a z-score, followed by probability subtraction and multiplication to find an expected frequency.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, standardise the given weight to a z-score using the formula \(z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\). Then, use the provided table to find the probability of being below this value, subtract from 1 to find the probability of being above it, and multiply by the total population size.

steps.title: Calculate the z-score
steps.bodyLatex: Standardise the weight \(x = 11.93\) using the mean \(\mu = 10.40\) and standard deviation \(\sigma = 1.15\). The formula is \(z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\). Substituting the values gives \(z = \frac{11.93 - 10.40}{1.15} = \frac{1.53}{1.15} = 1.33\).

steps.title: Find the probability from the table
steps.bodyLatex: The table provides the probability \(P(Z < z)\), which represents the area to the left of the z-score. For \(z = 1.33\), the table indicates the probability is \(0.9082\). This represents the proportion of koalas weighing less than \(11.93\text{ kg}\).

steps.title: Calculate the upper tail probability
steps.bodyLatex: Since we need the number of koalas weighing *more* than \(11.93\text{ kg}\), we look for the area to the right of the z-score. Using the complement rule, \(P(Z > 1.33) = 1 - P(Z < 1.33) = 1 - 0.9082 = 0.0918\).

steps.title: Determine the expected number
steps.bodyLatex: To find the expected number of koalas in a group of \(400\), multiply the total population by the probability: \(400 \times 0.0918 = 36.72\). Since we are counting individual koalas, we round to the nearest whole number, giving \(37\) koalas.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to subtract the table value from 1, which calculates the probability of being below the weight rather than above it.
Rounding the z-score incorrectly before looking it up in the table.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since \(11.93\) is more than one standard deviation above the mean (\(10.40 + 1.15 = 11.55\)), we expect the probability to be less than \(0.1587\) (the area beyond \(z=1\)), so \(0.0918\) is a reasonable result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Identify the equation of a linear function by determining its gradient and \(y\)-intercept from its graph.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Observe the direction of the line to determine the sign of the gradient, and identify where the line crosses the vertical axis to determine the sign of the \(y\)-intercept.

steps.title: Determine the gradient sign
steps.bodyLatex: Look at the slope of the line. Since the line falls from left to right, the gradient \(m\) must be negative. This eliminates options A and B, which have a positive gradient of \(2\).

steps.title: Determine the y-intercept sign
steps.bodyLatex: Identify the point where the line intersects the \(y\)-axis. The graph crosses the axis above the origin, meaning the \(y\)-intercept \(c\) is positive.

steps.title: Match with the general form
steps.bodyLatex: The equation of a line is given by \(y=mx+c\). We require a negative \(m\) and a positive \(c\).

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing our requirements to the remaining options, \(y=-2x+3\) has a gradient of \(-2\) and a \(y\)-intercept of \(3\). This matches the visual features of the graph.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the sign of the gradient by reading the graph from right to left.
Misinterpreting the \(y\)-intercept by looking at the \(x\)-intercept instead.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For \(y=-2x+3\), if \(x=0\), then \(y=3\), which is a positive intercept. If \(x=1\), then \(y=1\), confirming the line decreases as \(x\) increases.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the Principle of Inclusion-Exclusion for two sets to find the intersection of two groups.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, identify the total number of students who play at least one sport by subtracting those who play neither from the total group size. Then, use the inclusion-exclusion formula to find the overlap.

steps.title: Find the number of students playing at least one sport
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(B\) be the set of students who play basketball and \(H\) be the set of students who play hockey. Given that there are \(60\) students in total and \(5\) play neither, the number of students who play at least one sport is \(n(B \cup H) = 60 - 5 = 55\).

steps.title: Apply the Principle of Inclusion-Exclusion
steps.bodyLatex: The formula for the union of two sets is \(n(B \cup H) = n(B) + n(H) - n(B \cap H)\). We know \(n(B) = 38\), \(n(H) = 35\), and \(n(B \cup H) = 55\). We need to solve for \(n(B \cap H)\), which represents the students who play both sports.

steps.title: Rearrange and solve for the intersection
steps.bodyLatex: Rearranging the formula gives \(n(B \cap H) = n(B) + n(H) - n(B \cup H)\). Substituting the known values, we get \(n(B \cap H) = 38 + 35 - 55\).

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.bodyLatex: Performing the arithmetic, \(38 + 35 = 73\), and \(73 - 55 = 18\). Thus, \(18\) students play both basketball and hockey.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to subtract the 5 students who play neither sport, leading to the incorrect calculation 38 + 35 - 60 = 13.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the sum: \(n(B \text{ only}) = 38 - 18 = 20\), \(n(H \text{ only}) = 35 - 18 = 17\), \(n(\text{both}) = 18\), \(n(\text{neither}) = 5\). Total: \(20 + 17 + 18 + 5 = 60\). This matches the original total.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: To compare performance across different normally distributed datasets, calculate the \(z\)-score for each subject.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: The \(z\)-score measures how many standard deviations a score is above or below the mean. The formula is \(z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\), where \(x\) is the mark, \(\mu\) is the mean, and \(\sigma\) is the standard deviation. A higher \(z\)-score indicates a better relative performance.

steps.title: Calculate English z-score
steps.bodyLatex: For English, the mark is \(78\), the mean is \(66\), and the standard deviation is \(6\). The \(z\)-score is \(z = \frac{78 - 66}{6} = \frac{12}{6} = 2.0\).

steps.title: Calculate Mathematics and Science z-scores
steps.bodyLatex: For Mathematics, \(z = \frac{80 - 71}{10} = \frac{9}{10} = 0.9\). For Science, \(z = \frac{77 - 70}{15} = \frac{7}{15} \approx 0.47\).

steps.title: Calculate History z-score
steps.bodyLatex: For History, the mark is \(85\), the mean is \(72\), and the standard deviation is \(9\). The \(z\)-score is \(z = \frac{85 - 72}{9} = \frac{13}{9} \approx 1.44\).

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing the calculated values: \(2.0 > 1.44 > 0.9 > 0.47\). Since English has the highest \(z\)-score, Pia performed best in English relative to her cohort.

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming the highest raw mark (85 in History) implies the best performance, ignoring the mean and standard deviation.
Mixing up the numerator and denominator in the \(z\)-score formula.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The \(z\)-score represents the number of standard deviations from the mean. A score of \(2\) is significantly higher than the others, confirming it is the best relative result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves applying sequential geometric transformations to a quadratic function.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recognise that a reflection in the \(y\)-axis requires replacing \(x\) with \(-x\), and a reflection in the \(x\)-axis requires multiplying the entire function by \(-1\).

steps.title: Reflect in the y-axis
steps.bodyLatex: To reflect the function \(y = (x-3)^2 - 2\) about the \(y\)-axis, we replace \(x\) with \(-x\). This gives \(y = ((-x)-3)^2 - 2\). Since \((-x-3)^2 = (-(x+3))^2 = (x+3)^2\), the equation simplifies to \(y = (x+3)^2 - 2\).

steps.title: Reflect in the x-axis
steps.bodyLatex: To reflect the resulting function \(y = (x+3)^2 - 2\) about the \(x\)-axis, we multiply the entire expression by \(-1\). This transforms the function to \(y = -((x+3)^2 - 2)\).

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.bodyLatex: Distribute the negative sign across the terms inside the parentheses: \(y = -(x+3)^2 - (-2)\), which simplifies to \(y = -(x+3)^2 + 2\).

steps.title: Verify against options
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing our result \(y = -(x+3)^2 + 2\) to the given options, we see it matches option C.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to negate the constant term when reflecting about the x-axis.
Incorrectly simplifying \((-x-3)^2\) as \(-(x+3)^2\) instead of \((x+3)^2\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Test a point: the vertex of the original parabola is \((3, -2)\). After reflecting in the \(y\)-axis, it moves to \((-3, -2)\). After reflecting in the \(x\)-axis, it moves to \((-3, 2)\). Our final equation \(y = -(x+3)^2 + 2\) has a vertex at \((-3, 2)\), which confirms the result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem requires finding the indefinite integral of a composite function of the form \((ax+b)^n\).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recognise that the expression is a linear function raised to a power. You can use the reverse chain rule or a simple substitution to account for the derivative of the inner function.

steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.bodyLatex: The integral is of the form \(\int (ax+b)^n \, dx\). The standard rule for integrating this is \(\frac{(ax+b)^{n+1}}{a(n+1)} + C\), where \(a\) is the coefficient of \(x\) inside the brackets.

steps.title: Apply the integration rule
steps.bodyLatex: Here, \(a=6\) and \(n=3\). Applying the rule, we increase the power by one to get \((6x+1)^4\) and divide by the new power, which is \(4\). We must also divide by the derivative of the inner function, which is \(6\).

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.bodyLatex: Combining these factors, we get \(\frac{(6x+1)^4}{4 \times 6} + C\). Multiplying the denominator gives \(\frac{(6x+1)^4}{24} + C\), which is equivalent to \(\frac{1}{24}(6x+1)^4 + C\).

steps.title: Verify by differentiation
steps.bodyLatex: To check, differentiate \(\frac{1}{24}(6x+1)^4\) using the chain rule: \(\frac{1}{24} \times 4(6x+1)^3 \times 6\). Since \(\frac{24}{24} = 1\), this simplifies to \((6x+1)^3\), confirming the result is correct.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to divide by the derivative of the inner function (the coefficient 6), which leads to the incorrect answer B.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Differentiating the result should return the original integrand. Since \(\frac{d}{dx} [\frac{1}{24}(6x+1)^4] = \frac{4}{24}(6x+1)^3 \cdot 6 = (6x+1)^3\), the answer is verified.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: To find the domain of a function involving a square root in the denominator, we must ensure the radicand is strictly positive.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that the function \(f(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^2-1}}\) has two constraints: the expression inside the square root must be non-negative for the root to be defined, and the denominator cannot be zero. Combining these, the expression \(x^2-1\) must be strictly greater than zero.

steps.title: Identify the constraints
steps.bodyLatex: For the function to be defined, the expression inside the square root, \(x^2-1\), must be greater than or equal to zero. However, because this expression is in the denominator, it cannot equal zero. Therefore, we require the strict inequality \(x^2-1 > 0\).

steps.title: Solve the inequality
steps.bodyLatex: Factor the quadratic expression: \((x-1)(x+1) > 0\). This is a parabola opening upwards with roots at \(x=-1\) and \(x=1\). The expression is positive when \(x\) is outside the interval between the roots.

steps.title: Determine the intervals
steps.bodyLatex: The inequality \((x-1)(x+1) > 0\) holds when \(x < -1\) or \(x > 1\). These values ensure that \(x^2-1\) is positive, making the square root real and non-zero.

steps.title: Express in interval notation
steps.bodyLatex: Translating the inequalities \(x < -1\) and \(x > 1\) into interval notation gives \((-\infty, -1) \cup (1, \infty)\). This corresponds to option D.

commonMistakesLatex: Including the endpoints \(-1\) and \(1\) by using square brackets, which would cause division by zero.
Incorrectly identifying the region where the quadratic is positive, such as choosing the interval between the roots.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Test a value like \(x=0\): \(0^2-1 = -1\), which is negative, so \(0\) is not in the domain. Test \(x=2\): \(2^2-1 = 3\), which is positive, so \(2\) is in the domain.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question requires identifying the horizontal transformations applied to a function \(y=f(x)\) to produce \(y=f(2x-1)\).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To determine the transformation, rewrite the argument in the form \(f(k(x-h))\). This reveals the horizontal scaling factor and the horizontal shift clearly.

steps.title: Factorise the argument
steps.bodyLatex: Start by rewriting the function as \(y=f(2(x-\frac{1}{2}))\). This form is essential because it separates the horizontal dilation from the horizontal translation.

steps.title: Identify the horizontal dilation
steps.bodyLatex: The factor of \(2\) inside the function indicates a horizontal compression by a factor of \(\frac{1}{2}\). Every \(x\)-coordinate on the original graph is now halved.

steps.title: Identify the horizontal shift
steps.bodyLatex: The term \((x-\frac{1}{2})\) indicates a horizontal shift to the right by \(\frac{1}{2}\) units. Combining this with the compression, any point \((x, y)\) on the original graph moves to the new position \((\frac{x}{2} + \frac{1}{2}, y)\).

steps.title: Apply to key points
steps.bodyLatex: Test the transformation on key features like the zeros. If the original graph has zeros at \(x=0\), \(x=2\), and \(x=4\), the new zeros will be at \(\frac{0}{2} + 0.5 = 0.5\), \(\frac{2}{2} + 0.5 = 1.5\), and \(\frac{4}{2} + 0.5 = 2.5\).

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming the shift is \(1\) unit to the right. You must factorise the coefficient of \(x\) first to see the true horizontal shift.
Confusing horizontal compression with expansion. A factor \(k > 1\) inside the function always compresses the graph horizontally.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the transformation by substituting \(x=0.5\) into \(f(2x-1)\), which gives \(f(2(0.5)-1) = f(0)\). This confirms the original zero at \(x=0\) has moved to \(x=0.5\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question requires identifying inconsistencies between a box plot's five-number summary and the frequency distribution shown in a histogram.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Examine the box plot to identify the median, quartiles, and range. Then, evaluate each histogram to see if the distribution of data points aligns with these key statistics.

steps.title: Analyse the box plot
steps.bodyLatex: Identify the five-number summary from the box plot: the minimum, the first quartile \(Q_1\), the median \(Q_2\), the third quartile \(Q_3\), and the maximum. Note the skewness of the data by observing the relative lengths of the whiskers and the position of the median within the box.

steps.title: Interpret the histogram properties
steps.bodyLatex: A histogram represents the frequency of data in specific intervals. For a histogram to be compatible with a box plot, the area under the histogram must be divided such that 25\% of the total frequency lies between the minimum and \(Q_1\), 25\% between \(Q_1\) and the median, 25\% between the median and \(Q_3\), and 25\% between \(Q_3\) and the maximum.

steps.title: Evaluate the options
steps.bodyLatex: Check each histogram for consistency. If a histogram shows a high frequency of data in a region where the box plot indicates a low density (or vice versa), that histogram is likely incompatible. Specifically, look for cases where the median or quartiles of the histogram's distribution do not align with the box plot's markers.

steps.title: Identify the mismatch
steps.bodyLatex: Compare the distribution shape of option D against the box plot. If the histogram in option D suggests a concentration of data that contradicts the position of the median or the spread of the interquartile range shown in the box plot, it is the correct choice for the 'NOT possible' histogram.

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming the shape of the histogram must perfectly mirror the box plot's box, rather than checking if the quartiles align with the cumulative frequency.
Misreading the scale on the axes of the histogram, leading to an incorrect calculation of the median position.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify that the median of the histogram (the point where 50\% of the total frequency is reached) matches the vertical line inside the box of the box plot.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the definition of conditional probability, \(P(A|B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}\), where \(A\) is the event of selecting two red marbles and \(B\) is the event of selecting at least one red marble.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that 'selecting two marbles at the same time' is equivalent to selecting two marbles without replacement. We need to calculate the probability of the intersection (two red) and the probability of the condition (at least one red) separately.

steps.title: Calculate the probability of two red marbles
steps.bodyLatex: The probability of picking the first red marble is \(\frac{2}{5}\). Since we do not replace it, the probability of picking a second red marble is \(\frac{1}{4}\). Thus, the probability of selecting two red marbles is \(P(RR) = \frac{2}{5} \times \frac{1}{4} = \frac{2}{20} = \frac{1}{10}\).

steps.title: Calculate the probability of at least one red marble
steps.bodyLatex: It is easier to use the complement rule. The complement of 'at least one red' is 'both are white'. The probability of picking two white marbles is \(P(WW) = \frac{3}{5} \times \frac{2}{4} = \frac{6}{20} = \frac{3}{10}\). Therefore, the probability of at least one red marble is \(P(\text{at least one } R) = 1 - P(WW) = 1 - \frac{3}{10} = \frac{7}{10}\).

steps.title: Apply the conditional probability formula
steps.bodyLatex: We want the probability that both are red, given that at least one is red. This is defined as \(P(RR | \text{at least one } R) = \frac{P(RR \cap \text{at least one } R)}{P(\text{at least one } R)}\). Since the event 'both are red' is a subset of 'at least one is red', the intersection is simply \(P(RR)\).

steps.title: Final calculation
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the values calculated in the previous steps: \(\frac{P(RR)}{P(\text{at least one } R)} = \frac{1/10}{7/10}\). Simplifying this fraction gives \(\frac{1}{10} \times \frac{10}{7} = \frac{1}{7}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that the denominator for conditional probability is the probability of the condition, not the total sample space.
Assuming the events are independent, which would lead to incorrect probabilities for the second marble.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The total probability space consists of \( inom{5}{2} = 10\) possible pairs. The pairs are: 1 RR, 6 RW, and 3 WW. Given at least one red, we are restricted to the 1 RR and 6 RW pairs (7 total). The probability of RR is \(\frac{1}{7}\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to relate the inflection points of an integral function to the turning points of its integrand.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recognise that for \(A(x) = \int_0^x f(t) \, dt\), the second derivative is \(A''(x) = f'(x)\). Inflection points of \(A(x)\) occur where \(A''(x) = 0\) and changes sign, which is equivalent to where \(f'(x) = 0\) and changes sign—the turning points of \(f(x)\).

steps.title: Relate derivatives
steps.bodyLatex: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, the first derivative is \(A'(x) = f(x)\). Differentiating again gives \(A''(x) = f'(x)\). Therefore, the inflection points of \(A(x)\) occur exactly where the gradient of \(f(x)\) changes sign.

steps.title: Identify turning points
steps.bodyLatex: A point of inflection for \(A(x)\) exists where \(f'(x)\) changes sign. This occurs at the local maximums and local minimums of the graph \(y = f(x)\). Looking at the graph, there are three distinct turning points where the slope of \(f(x)\) changes from positive to negative or vice versa.

steps.title: Analyse point Q
steps.bodyLatex: The point \(Q\) is a horizontal point of inflection for \(f(x)\). At \(Q\), the gradient \(f'(x)\) is zero, but it does not change sign (it remains positive or negative on both sides). Since \(A''(x) = f'(x)\) does not change sign at \(Q\), \(A(x)\) does not have an inflection point at the \(x\)-coordinate of \(Q\$.

steps.title: Count the points
steps.bodyLatex: Since there are exactly three turning points on the graph of \(f(x)\) where the gradient changes sign, there are three values of \(x\) where \(A''(x)\) changes sign. Thus, the graph of \(y = A(x)\) has exactly 3 points of inflection.

commonMistakesLatex: Mistaking a horizontal point of inflection on \(f(x)\) for a turning point. Remember that \(A''(x)\) must change sign for an inflection point to exist.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify that the number of inflection points of the integral function matches the number of local extrema of the integrand function.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem requires identifying the slope and concavity of a function \(g(x)\) from its graph to determine the signs of its first and second derivatives.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recall that the first derivative \(g'(x)\) represents the gradient (slope) and the second derivative \(g''(x)\) represents the concavity. Look for whether the graph is increasing/decreasing for \(g'(x)\), and whether it is concave up/down for \(g''(x)\).

steps.title: Analyse x = -3
steps.bodyLatex: At \(x=-3\), the graph is sloping upwards, meaning the gradient is positive, so \(g'(-3) > 0\). The shape of the curve is 'frowning' or concave down, which indicates that the second derivative is negative, so \(g''(-3) < 0\).

steps.title: Analyse x = 1
steps.bodyLatex: At \(x=1\), the graph has a stationary point where the tangent is horizontal, so \(g'(1) = 0\). Because the curve flattens out at this point of inflection before continuing to increase, the concavity is zero, meaning \(g''(1) = 0\).

steps.title: Analyse x = 5
steps.bodyLatex: At \(x=5\), the graph is sloping upwards, so the first derivative is positive, \(g'(5) > 0\). The curve is 'smiling' or concave up, which indicates that the second derivative is positive, so \(g''(5) > 0\).

steps.title: Summary Table
steps.bodyLatex: Consolidate the findings: for \(x=-3\), first derivative is positive and second derivative is negative. For \(x=1\), both are zero. For \(x=5\), both are positive.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: The table entries are: \(x=-3\) (positive, negative); \(x=1\) (zero, zero); \(x=5\) (positive, positive).
commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the sign of the second derivative: remember that concave down (frown) corresponds to a negative second derivative, while concave up (smile) corresponds to a positive one.
Assuming a stationary point always has a non-zero second derivative; at a horizontal point of inflection, the second derivative is zero.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the signs against the visual shape: increasing slopes are positive, decreasing slopes are negative, 'cups' are positive concavity, and 'caps' are negative concavity.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem requires identifying the parameters of an arithmetic series and using the sum formula to calculate the total.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, identify the first term \(a\) and the common difference \(d\). Since the number of terms \(n\) is unknown, use the general term formula \(T_n = a + (n-1)d\) to solve for \(n\) using the last term.

steps.title: Identify the series parameters
steps.bodyLatex: From the series \(50+57+64+\cdots+2024\), we identify the first term \(a = 50\) and the common difference \(d = 57 - 50 = 7\). The last term is \(T_n = 2024\).

steps.title: Calculate the number of terms
steps.bodyLatex: Use the formula for the \(n\)-th term of an arithmetic sequence: \(T_n = a + (n-1)d\). Substitute the known values: \(2024 = 50 + (n-1)7\). Subtract \(50\) from both sides to get \(1974 = (n-1)7\), then divide by \(7\) to find \(n-1 = 282\), which gives \(n = 283\).

steps.title: Apply the sum formula
steps.bodyLatex: The sum of an arithmetic series is given by \(S_n = \frac{n}{2}(a + l)\), where \(l\) is the last term. Substituting our values: \(S_{283} = \frac{283}{2}(50 + 2024)\).

steps.title: Final calculation
steps.bodyLatex: Simplify the expression inside the brackets: \(S_{283} = \frac{283}{2}(2074)\). Dividing \(2074\) by \(2\) gives \(1037\). Finally, calculate \(283 \times 1037 = 293471\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to add 1 when solving for \(n\), leading to an incorrect number of terms.
Using the wrong sum formula or misidentifying the last term \(l\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check that \(n\) is a positive integer. Since \(n=283\), the calculation is consistent with the series structure.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves interpreting exponential growth and decay models of the form \(y=ab^x\).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the initial values (\(x=0\)) and the growth/decay factors from the given equations. Use the provided data points to solve for missing variables and calculate future predictions.

steps.title: Determine initial values
steps.bodyLatex: The initial population occurs at \(x=0\) (the year 1985). For population \(W\), \(A=34\). For population \(K\), we are given the predicted population \(y=61\) at \(x=50\). Using the model \(y=B(0.97)^x\), we solve \(61=B(0.97)^{50}\). Calculating \(0.97^{50} \approx 0.218\), we find \(B = \frac{61}{0.97^{50}} \approx 280.05\). Thus, \(B=280\).

steps.title: Interpret percentage change
steps.bodyLatex: The base of an exponential model \(b\) relates to the percentage change \(r\) by the formula \(b = 1 + r\). For population \(W\), \(b=1.055\), so \(1+r=1.055\), giving \(r=0.055\), which is a \(5.5\%\) increase. For population \(K\), \(b=0.97\), so \(1+r=0.97\), giving \(r=-0.03\), which is a \(3\%\) decrease.

steps.title: Predict future population
steps.bodyLatex: To find the population of \(W\) when \(x=50\), substitute \(x=50\) into the model \(y=34(1.055)^x\). This gives \(y = 34(1.055)^{50}\). Using a calculator, \(1.055^{50} \approx 14.542\), so \(y \approx 34 \times 14.542 \approx 494.43\).

steps.title: Finalise the table
steps.bodyLatex: Rounding the population to the nearest whole number as is standard for animal populations, we conclude the predicted population \(W\) is \(494\). The table is now complete with \(B=280\), the percentage changes identified, and the final prediction calculated.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: The completed values are \(B=280\), population \(W\) has a \(5.5\%\) increase, population \(K\) has a \(3\%\) decrease, and the predicted population \(W\) at \(x=50\) is \(494\).
commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the growth factor \(b\) with the percentage rate \(r\). Remember that \(r = b - 1\).
Rounding too early in the calculation for \(B\), which can lead to an inaccurate final result.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the decay: \(280 \times (0.97)^{50} \approx 61\). The model is consistent with the provided data.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem requires finding the intersection points of two functions and calculating the area between them using a definite integral.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To find the intersection points, equate the two functions. To find the area, integrate the difference between the upper curve and the lower curve over the interval defined by the intersection points.

steps.title: Find intersection points
steps.bodyLatex: Equate the two functions: \((x-1)^2 = 5-x^2\). Expand the left side to get \(x^2 - 2x + 1 = 5 - x^2\). Rearrange all terms to one side to form a quadratic equation: \(2x^2 - 2x - 4 = 0\). Dividing by \(2\) gives \(x^2 - x - 2 = 0\). Factoring this quadratic yields \((x-2)(x+1) = 0\), so the curves intersect at \(x = -1\) and \(x = 2\).

steps.title: Identify the upper curve
steps.bodyLatex: Test a value between \(-1\) and \(2\), such as \(x=0\). At \(x=0\), the first curve is \(y=(0-1)^2=1\) and the second is \(y=5-0^2=5\). Since \(5 > 1\), the curve \(y=5-x^2\) is the upper boundary of the shaded region. The integrand for the area is therefore \((5-x^2) - (x-1)^2\).

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.bodyLatex: Simplify the integrand before integrating: \(5 - x^2 - (x^2 - 2x + 1) = 5 - x^2 - x^2 + 2x - 1 = 4 + 2x - 2x^2\). The area \(A\) is given by the definite integral: \(A = \int_{-1}^{2} (4 + 2x - 2x^2) \, dx\).

steps.title: Evaluate the integral
steps.bodyLatex: Find the primitive: \(\left[ 4x + x^2 - \frac{2x^3}{3} \right]_{-1}^{2}\). Substitute the upper limit \(x=2\): \(4(2) + (2)^2 - \frac{2(8)}{3} = 8 + 4 - \frac{16}{3} = 12 - \frac{16}{3} = \frac{20}{3}\). Substitute the lower limit \(x=-1\): \(4(-1) + (-1)^2 - \frac{2(-1)}{3} = -4 + 1 + \frac{2}{3} = -3 + \frac{2}{3} = -\frac{7}{3}\). Subtracting gives \(\frac{20}{3} - (-\frac{7}{3}) = \frac{27}{3} = 9\).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) The intersection points are \(x=-1\) and \(x=2\). (b) The area is \(9\) square units.
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to simplify the integrand before integrating, which often leads to sign errors.
Subtracting the curves in the wrong order, resulting in a negative area value.
Arithmetic errors when evaluating the definite integral at the boundaries.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since the area must be positive, ensure your final result is a positive value. A quick sketch confirms the region is above the x-axis and bounded correctly.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses integration to find the volume function from a rate of change, then evaluates it at a specific time.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that you are given the rate of change \(\frac{dV}{dt}\). To find the volume \(V\), integrate the rate with respect to time \(t\), use the initial condition to find \(C\), then solve for \(t\) when the rate is zero to find the final volume.

steps.title: Integrate the rate function
steps.bodyLatex: Integrate the given rate: \[V(t)=\int(300-7.5t)\,dt=300t-\frac{7.5t^2}{2}+C=300t-3.75t^2+C.\]

steps.title: Determine the constant C
steps.bodyLatex:
Use the initial condition that at \(t = 0\), the volume \(V = 350\). Substitute these values into your expression: 
\[ 350 = 300(0) - 3.75(0)^2 + C \implies C = 350 \] 
So, the volume function is \(V(t) = 300t - 3.75t^2 + 350\).

steps.title: Find the time when the rate is zero
steps.bodyLatex: Set the rate equal to zero: \[300-7.5t=0\implies 7.5t=300\implies t=\frac{300}{7.5}=40.\] So the tank stops increasing after \(40\) hours.

steps.title: Calculate the final volume
steps.bodyLatex:
Substitute \(t = 40\) into the volume function \(V(t)\) to find the total amount of water: 
\[ V(40) = 300(40) - 3.75(40)^2 + 350 \] 
\[ V(40) = 12000 - 3.75(1600) + 350 = 12000 - 6000 + 350 = 6350 \]

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: The volume of water in the tank is \(6350\) litres.
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to include the constant of integration \(C\) or failing to use the initial condition \(V(0)=350\) to solve for it.
Confusing the time \(t\) at which the rate is zero with the volume \(V\) itself.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the rate at \(t=40\): \(300 - 7.5(40) = 300 - 300 = 0\). The calculation is consistent.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question requires a comparative analysis of two datasets using the visual features of parallel box-plots.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To compare distributions, examine the three key statistical features: skewness (determined by whisker length and median position), central tendency (the median), and spread (the interquartile range and total range).

steps.title: Analyse skewness
steps.bodyLatex: Skewness is identified by the relative lengths of the whiskers and the position of the median within the box. For Garden A, the longer lower whisker indicates negative skewness. For Garden B, the longer upper whisker indicates positive skewness.

steps.title: Compare central tendency
steps.bodyLatex: The central tendency is represented by the vertical line inside each box, which marks the median. By observing the horizontal axis, compare the position of these lines. Garden A has a median height that is clearly positioned to the right of Garden B's median, indicating a higher median height.

steps.title: Compare spread
steps.bodyLatex: Spread is measured by the interquartile range (the width of the box) and the total range (the distance from the end of the left whisker to the end of the right whisker). Garden A has a wider box and a greater total length than Garden B, showing that its heights are more variable.

steps.title: Synthesise the comparison
steps.bodyLatex: Combine these observations into a coherent statement. Ensure you explicitly mention all three required aspects: skewness, central tendency, and spread, while referencing both Garden A and Garden B to provide a complete comparison.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: Garden A is negatively skewed, while Garden B is positively skewed. Garden A has a higher median height. Garden A also has a larger interquartile range and a larger overall range, indicating that its flower heights are more spread out than those in Garden B.
commonMistakesLatex: Describing only one garden instead of comparing both.
Confusing the interquartile range (the box width) with the total range (the full length of the plot).
Misinterpreting the direction of skewness by looking at the box instead of the whiskers.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ensure the comparison uses comparative language such as 'higher', 'larger', or 'more spread out' rather than just stating the values for each garden individually.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves sketching an exponential growth function and applying differentiation to determine a rate of change.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recognise that \(V(t)\) is a transformation of the exponential decay function. For the sketch, identify the intercept and the horizontal asymptote. For the calculations, use algebraic manipulation of logarithms and the chain rule for differentiation.

steps.title: Sketching the function
steps.bodyLatex: At \(t=0\), \(V(0)=6.5(1-e^0)=0\), so the graph starts at the origin \((0,0)\). As \(t \to \infty\), \(e^{-kt} \to 0\), meaning \(V(t) \to 6.5\). The graph is strictly increasing and concave down, approaching the horizontal asymptote \(V=6.5\).

steps.title: Solving for the constant k
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute \(t=1\) and \(V=2.6\) into the equation: \(2.6 = 6.5(1 - e^{-k})\). Divide by \(6.5\) to get \(0.4 = 1 - e^{-k}\), which simplifies to \(e^{-k} = 0.6\). Taking the natural logarithm of both sides gives \(-k = \ln(0.6)\), so \(k = -\ln(0.6) \approx 0.5108256\).

steps.title: Finding the rate of change
steps.bodyLatex: To find the rate of change, differentiate \(V(t) = 6.5 - 6.5e^{-kt}\) with respect to \(t\). Using the chain rule, \(\frac{dV}{dt} = -6.5 \times (-k)e^{-kt} = 6.5ke^{-kt}\).

steps.title: Calculating the final value
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute \(t=2\) and the value of \(k\) into the derivative: \(\frac{dV}{dt} = 6.5(0.5108256)e^{-2(0.5108256)}\). Evaluating this expression yields \(\frac{dV}{dt} \approx 3.320366 \times e^{-1.02165} \approx 1.195\) volts per second.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) The graph starts at \((0,0)\) and approaches \(V=6.5\). (b) \(k \approx 0.511\). (c) The rate is approximately \(1.195 \text{ V/s}\).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the chain rule when differentiating \(e^{-kt}\), which results in missing the factor of \(-k\).
Rounding the value of \(k\) too early, which can lead to an inaccurate final answer for the rate of change.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the rate of change by observing that as \(t\) increases, the rate \(\frac{dV}{dt} = 6.5ke^{-kt}\) should decrease, which is consistent with the graph flattening out towards the asymptote.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the complement rule for independent events and logarithmic inequalities to solve for the number of trials.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recognise that each attempt is an independent Bernoulli trial. For part (a), use the complement of scoring. For part (b), use the complement rule \(P(\text{at least one}) = 1 - P(\text{none})\) to set up an exponential inequality.

steps.title: Calculate probability of missing
steps.bodyLatex: Since the probability of scoring a goal is \(p = 0.15\), the probability of not scoring (missing) in a single attempt is \(1 - 0.15 = 0.85\). Because each attempt is independent, the probability of missing two consecutive attempts is \(0.85 \times 0.85 = (0.85)^2\).

steps.title: Solve part (a)
steps.bodyLatex: Calculating the value from the previous step, we find the probability that the player does not score a goal in the first two attempts is \((0.85)^2 = 0.7225\).

steps.title: Set up the inequality for part (b)
steps.bodyLatex: We want the probability of at least one goal in \(n\) attempts to be greater than \(0.8\). Using the complement, \(P(\text{at least one}) = 1 - P(\text{no goals}) = 1 - (0.85)^n\). We set up the inequality \(1 - (0.85)^n > 0.8\), which simplifies to \((0.85)^n < 0.2\).

steps.title: Solve for n using logarithms
steps.bodyLatex: Take the natural logarithm of both sides: \(\ln((0.85)^n) < \ln(0.2)\), which becomes \(n \ln(0.85) < \ln(0.2)\). Since \(\ln(0.85)\) is negative, dividing by it reverses the inequality sign: \(n > \frac{\ln(0.2)}{\ln(0.85)}\). Calculating this gives \(n > 9.90... \). Since \(n\) must be an integer, the least number of attempts is \(10\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to reverse the inequality sign when dividing by \(\ln(0.85)\), which is a negative value.
Rounding \(9.90\) down to \(9\) instead of up to \(10\); since we need the probability to be *greater* than \(0.8\), \(9\) attempts are insufficient.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the result: for \(n=9\), \(1-(0.85)^9 \approx 0.768 < 0.8\). For \(n=10\), \(1-(0.85)^{10} \approx 0.803 > 0.8\). The answer is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves using the first and second derivatives to identify stationary points, their nature, and points of inflection for a quartic curve.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To sketch the curve, we must find the stationary points by solving \(y'=0\) and determine their nature using the second derivative test. We then find points of inflection by solving \(y''=0\) and checking for a change in concavity.

steps.title: Find stationary points
steps.bodyLatex: Differentiate the function to get \(y'=4x^3-6x^2\). Set \(y'=0\) to find stationary points: \(2x^2(2x-3)=0\). This gives \(x=0\) and \(x=\frac{3}{2}\). Substituting these into the original equation, we find the points are \((0,2)\) and \(\left(\frac{3}{2}, \frac{5}{16}\right)\).

steps.title: Determine the nature of stationary points
steps.bodyLatex: Find the second derivative: \(y''=12x^2-12x\). At \(x=\frac{3}{2}\), \(y''=12(\frac{9}{4})-12(\frac{3}{2}) = 27-18=9\). Since \(y''>0\), the point \(\left(\frac{3}{2}, \frac{5}{16}\right)\) is a local minimum. At \(x=0\), \(y''=0\), so the second derivative test is inconclusive; however, checking the sign of \(y'\) around \(x=0\) shows it remains negative, confirming \((0,2)\) is a horizontal point of inflection.

steps.title: Find points of inflection
steps.bodyLatex: Set \(y''=0\) to find potential points of inflection: \(12x(x-1)=0\), giving \(x=0\) and \(x=1\). We already know \((0,2)\) is a point of inflection. For \(x=1\), \(y=1-2+2=1\). Since \(y''\) changes sign at \(x=1\) (from negative to positive), \((1,1)\) is a point of inflection.

steps.title: Sketch the curve
steps.bodyLatex: Plot the points \((0,2)\), \((1,1)\), and \(\left(\frac{3}{2}, \frac{5}{16}\right)\). The curve starts from positive infinity, passes through the horizontal inflection at \((0,2)\), curves downwards through \((1,1)\) to the local minimum at \(\left(\frac{3}{2}, \frac{5}{16}\right)\), and then rises towards positive infinity.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: Stationary points: \((0,2)\) (horizontal inflection) and \(\left(\frac{3}{2}, \frac{5}{16}\right)\) (local minimum). Points of inflection: \((0,2)\) and \((1,1)\).
commonMistakesLatex: Assuming that \(y''=0\) automatically means a point of inflection; you must verify that the concavity actually changes sign.
Forgetting to check the sign of the first derivative on either side of \(x=0\) to confirm the nature of the stationary point when the second derivative test is inconclusive.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: As \(x \to \pm\infty\), \(y \to \infty\), which is consistent with a positive quartic coefficient.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses right-angled trigonometry to find horizontal distances, followed by the cosine rule to determine the angle between two points on a horizontal plane.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, identify two right-angled triangles sharing the vertical height of the tower. Use the tangent ratio to find the horizontal distances \(AC\) and \(BC\). Then, use the cosine rule in the horizontal triangle \( riangle ABC\) to find the angle \(\angle ACB\).

steps.title: Calculate horizontal distances
steps.bodyLatex: In the right-angled triangles \( riangle TCA\) and \( riangle TCB\), the tower height \(TC = 40\) m is the opposite side to the angles of elevation. Using \(\tan\theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}\), we find \(AC = \frac{40}{\tan 35^\circ} \approx 57.13\) m and \(BC = \frac{40}{\tan 30^\circ} \approx 69.28\) m.

steps.title: Apply the cosine rule
steps.bodyLatex: In the horizontal triangle \( riangle ABC\), we know all three side lengths: \(AC \approx 57.13\), \(BC \approx 69.28\), and \(AB = 100\). To find \(\angle ACB\), use the cosine rule: \(\cos(\angle ACB) = \frac{AC^2 + BC^2 - AB^2}{2(AC)(BC)}\).

steps.title: Solve for the angle
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the values: \(\cos(\angle ACB) = \frac{57.13^2 + 69.28^2 - 100^2}{2 \times 57.13 \times 69.28} \approx \frac{3263.8 + 4799.7 - 10000}{7916.3} \approx -0.2429\). Taking the inverse cosine, \(\angle ACB \approx \arccos(-0.2429) \approx 104.06^\circ\).

steps.title: Determine the bearing
steps.bodyLatex: Point \(A\) is due east of \(C\), which corresponds to a bearing of \(090^\circ\). Since \(B\) is \(104^\circ\) further clockwise from the line \(CA\), the bearing of \(B\) from \(C\) is \(90^\circ + 104^\circ = 194^\circ\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the angle of elevation with the angle of depression.
Rounding intermediate values too early, which leads to inaccuracy in the final angle.
Incorrectly interpreting the bearing relative to the line \(CA\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check that \( riangle ABC\) satisfies the triangle inequality: \(57.13 + 69.28 > 100\), which is \(126.41 > 100\). This confirms the triangle is valid.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question requires interpreting a bivariate scatterplot to identify trends, associations, and differences between two groups.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: When analysing a scatterplot, look for the overall trend (positive or negative correlation), the relative position of data clusters (which group is higher), and the rate of change (steepness of the points).

steps.title: Compare the growth rates
steps.bodyLatex: Observe the steepness of the data points for each gender. The female data points show a steeper positive gradient compared to the male data points as age increases. This indicates that female anacondas grow in length at a faster rate than male anacondas.

steps.title: Examine growth after maturity
steps.bodyLatex: The question notes that maturity is reached at approximately \(4\) years of age. By looking at the scatterplot for ages greater than \(4\), we can see that the length values continue to increase for both groups, meaning growth does not stop at maturity.

steps.title: Compare overall length
steps.bodyLatex: Look at the vertical distribution of the points. For any given age on the horizontal axis, the cluster of points representing female anacondas is generally positioned higher than the cluster representing male anacondas. This suggests that female anacondas are generally longer than males.

steps.title: Synthesise the observations
steps.bodyLatex: Combine these observations into three distinct points: female anacondas grow faster, both genders continue growing after \(4\) years, and females are generally longer than males.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: Three observations are: (1) Female anacondas grow at a faster rate than male anacondas; (2) Both female and male anacondas continue to grow after about \(4\) years of age; (3) Female anacondas are generally longer than male anacondas.
commonMistakesLatex: Describing the data as a perfect line; remember that scatterplots show trends, not exact functions.
Confusing the axes; ensure you are relating length (vertical) to age (horizontal).
Making claims that aren't supported by the plot, such as predicting the maximum possible length of an anaconda.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ensure each observation directly references the visual evidence provided by the scatterplot.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves using the second derivative to determine concavity and applying the trapezoidal rule to estimate an area under a symmetric curve.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), use the quotient rule to find the second derivative. For part (b), recognise the symmetry of the function about the \(y\)-axis to simplify the calculation. For part (c), relate the concavity found in part (a) to the geometric properties of the trapezoidal rule.

steps.title: Proving Concavity
steps.bodyLatex: To prove concavity, find the second derivative. Using the chain rule, \(f'(x) = \frac{2x}{1+x^2}\). Applying the quotient rule, \(f''(x) = \frac{2(1+x^2) - 2x(2x)}{(1+x^2)^2} = \frac{2-2x^2}{(1+x^2)^2} = \frac{2(1-x^2)}{(1+x^2)^2}\). For \(-1 < x < 1\), \(x^2 < 1\), so \(1-x^2 > 0\). Since the denominator is always positive, \(f''(x) > 0\), proving the function is concave up.

steps.title: Applying the Trapezoidal Rule
steps.bodyLatex: The shaded area is symmetric about \(x=0\), so we calculate the area from \(x=0\) to \(x=1\) and multiply by \(2\). With \(n=4\) sub-intervals, the width is \(h=0.25\). The rule is \(\text{Area} \approx 2 \times \frac{h}{2} [y_0 + y_4 + 2(y_1 + y_2 + y_3)]\). Substituting the values: \(0.25 \times [0 + 0.6931 + 2(0.0606 + 0.2231 + 0.4463)]\).

steps.title: Calculating the Estimate
steps.bodyLatex: Summing the values inside the brackets: \(0.0606 + 0.2231 + 0.4463 = 0.7300\). Then, \(2 \times 0.7300 = 1.4600\). Adding the endpoints: \(1.4600 + 0.6931 = 2.1531\). Finally, multiply by the width factor \(0.25\): \(0.25 \times 2.1531 = 0.538275\).

steps.title: Determining Overestimate or Underestimate
steps.bodyLatex: The trapezoidal rule approximates the area under a curve by connecting points on the curve with straight line segments. Because we proved in part (a) that the function is concave up (\(f''(x) > 0\)), these straight line segments lie entirely above the curve. Therefore, the trapezoidal rule provides an overestimate of the actual area.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) \(f''(x) = \frac{2(1-x^2)}{(1+x^2)^2} > 0\). (b) The estimated area is \(0.538275\). (c) It is an overestimate because the function is concave up.
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to multiply by 2 to account for the symmetry of the shaded region.
Confusing the concavity condition; remember that concave up means the curve bends upwards, so chords lie above the curve.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the symmetry: \(f(-x) = \ln(1+(-x)^2) = \ln(1+x^2) = f(x)\), confirming the function is even and the area calculation is valid.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the standard normal distribution formula \(z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\) and the symmetry of the normal curve to calculate probabilities and percentiles.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, convert raw scores to \(z\)-scores using the given mean \(\mu=58\) and standard deviation \(\sigma=15\). Use the symmetry property \(P(-z < Z < 0) = P(0 < Z < z)\) to simplify calculations, and use the provided table to find cumulative probabilities.

steps.title: Calculate the z-score for 70
steps.bodyLatex: To find the percentage of scores between \(58\) and \(70\), first convert \(x=70\) to a \(z\)-score: \(z = \frac{70 - 58}{15} = \frac{12}{15} = 0.8\). Since \(58\) is the mean, its \(z\)-score is \(0\). We need the area \(P(0 < Z < 0.8)\), which is \(P(Z < 0.8) - P(Z < 0) = 0.7881 - 0.5 = 0.2881\), or \(28.81\%\).

steps.title: Explain the symmetry for part (b)
steps.bodyLatex: The score \(46\) has a \(z\)-score of \(z = \frac{46 - 58}{15} = -0.8\). The interval \([46, 70]\) covers \(z\)-scores from \(-0.8\) to \(0.8\). Because the normal distribution is perfectly symmetric about the mean, the area between \(-0.8\) and \(0\) is identical to the area between \(0\) and \(0.8\). Thus, the total area is \(2 \times P(0 < Z < 0.8)\), which is twice the answer from part (a).

steps.title: Find the z-score for the top 10%
steps.bodyLatex: Being in the top \(10\%\) means the cumulative probability is \(P(Z < z) = 0.90\). Looking at the provided table, the value closest to \(0.90\) is \(0.9032\), which corresponds to \(z = 1.3\). This \(z\)-score represents the threshold where \(90\%\) of candidates scored lower and \(10\%\) scored higher.

steps.title: Convert back to a raw score
steps.bodyLatex: Use the formula \(x = \mu + z\sigma\) to convert the \(z\)-score back to the examination score scale: \(x = 58 + 1.3(15) = 58 + 19.5 = 77.5\). Since examination scores are typically whole numbers, we round this to \(78\) as the minimum score required to be in the top \(10\%\).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) \(28.81\%\), (b) The interval is symmetric about the mean, (c) \(78\).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to subtract \(0.5\) when looking up values in the table, as the table provides cumulative area from the left tail.
Confusing the standard normal variable \(Z\) with the raw score variable \(X\) when performing calculations.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check that \(z=0\) gives \(P(Z<0)=0.5\), which matches the table. Also, ensure \(z=1.3\) is reasonable for the top \(10\%\) (since \(z=1\) is roughly the top \(16\%\), \(z=1.3\) should be a smaller percentage).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the sum of a geometric series to model the future value of an annuity due.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that deposits are made at the start of each month, meaning the first deposit earns interest for the full 24 months. We treat this as a geometric series where the first term is \(80(1.005)\) and the common ratio is \(1.005\).

steps.title: Identify the parameters
steps.bodyLatex: The annual interest rate is \(6\%\), so the monthly rate is \(r = \frac{0.06}{12} = 0.005\). The number of periods is \(n = 24\). Because the deposit occurs at the start of the month, the first deposit of \(\$80\) earns interest for 24 months, the second for 23, and the final deposit for 1 month.

steps.title: Set up the geometric series
steps.bodyLatex: The total future value \(FV\) is the sum: \(FV = 80(1.005)^1 + 80(1.005)^2 + \dots + 80(1.005)^{24}\). This is a geometric series with first term \(a = 80(1.005)\), common ratio \(r = 1.005\), and \(n = 24\) terms.

steps.title: Calculate the future value
steps.bodyLatex: Using the sum formula \(S_n = \frac{a(r^n - 1)}{r - 1}\), we get \(FV = \frac{80(1.005)((1.005)^{24} - 1)}{1.005 - 1}\). Calculating this gives \(FV = \frac{80.4(1.127159776 - 1)}{0.005} \approx 2044.73\). Jack will have \(\$2044.73\) in his account.

steps.title: Find the annuity factor
steps.bodyLatex: The future value factor \(A\) represents the value of an annuity of \(\$1\) per period. Since \(FV = P \times A\), where \(P = 80\), we have \(A = \frac{2044.73}{80} \approx 25.5591\). Rounded to three decimal places, \(A = 25.559\).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) The account balance is \(\$2044.73\). (b) The table value is \(A \approx 25.559\).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that deposits at the start of the month earn interest for the final month, leading to an incorrect geometric series.
Using the standard annuity formula \(S_n = \frac{P((1+r)^n - 1)}{r}\) which assumes payments at the end of the period.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: A quick sanity check: if there were no interest, the total would be \(80 \times 24 = 1920\). Since interest is added, a result slightly higher than 1920 is expected.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem involves using the properties of a probability density function (PDF) to determine a constant, finding the cumulative distribution function (CDF), and solving for the median.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For a function to be a PDF, the total area under the curve must equal \(1\). Once \(h\) is found, integrate the PDF to find the CDF. The median is the value \(m\) where the CDF equals \(0.5\).

steps.title: Determine the constant h
steps.bodyLatex: The area under the PDF \(f(x)\) must be \(1\). Since \(f(x)\) forms a triangle with base \(h\) and height \(f(0) = 1\), we use the area formula for a triangle: \(\frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = 1\). Substituting the values gives \(\frac{1}{2} \times h \times 1 = 1\), which simplifies to \(h = 2\).

steps.title: Find the cumulative distribution function F(x)
steps.bodyLatex: The CDF \(F(x)\) is the integral of the PDF from the lower bound to \(x\). For \(0 \le x \le 2\), calculate \(F(x) = \int_0^x (1 - \frac{t}{2}) dt = \left[ t - \frac{t^2}{4} \right]_0^x = x - \frac{x^2}{4}\). Including the bounds for \(x < 0\) and \(x > 2\), the full CDF is defined piecewise.

steps.title: Sketch the CDF
steps.bodyLatex: The graph of \(F(x)\) is \(0\) for \(x < 0\), a concave-down parabola \(F(x) = x - \frac{x^2}{4}\) for \(0 \le x \le 2\), and a constant \(1\) for \(x > 2\). Plot the points \((0,0)\), \((1, 0.75)\), and \((2, 1)\) to show the curve connecting the horizontal lines.

steps.title: Calculate the median
steps.bodyLatex: The median \(m\) satisfies \(F(m) = 0.5\). Setting \(m - \frac{m^2}{4} = 0.5\) leads to the quadratic equation \(m^2 - 4m + 2 = 0\). Using the quadratic formula \(m = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}\), we get \(m = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 8}}{2} = 2 \pm \sqrt{2}\). Since \(0 \le m \le 2\), we choose \(m = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.586\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that the total area under a PDF must be 1.
Using the quadratic formula and failing to reject the root \(2+\sqrt{2}\) which lies outside the domain \([0, 2]\).
Confusing the PDF with the CDF when solving for the median.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check that \(F(2) = 2 - \frac{2^2}{4} = 2 - 1 = 1\), which confirms the CDF correctly reaches the total probability of 1 at the upper bound.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the present value of an annuity formula to calculate the initial deposit required to fund two different withdrawal streams.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recognise that the total withdrawal stream can be split into two simpler annuities: a constant monthly withdrawal of \(\$1200\) for the full \(300\) months, and an additional \(\$800\) withdrawal for the first \(180\) months.

steps.title: Determine the monthly interest rate
steps.bodyLatex: The annual interest rate is \(2.4\%\), compounded monthly. To find the monthly rate \(r\), divide the annual rate by \(12\): \(r = \frac{0.024}{12} = 0.002\).

steps.title: Decompose the withdrawal stream
steps.bodyLatex: Phoenix withdraws \(\$2000\) for \(180\) months and \(\$1200\) for the remaining \(120\) months. This is equivalent to a base annuity of \(\$1200\) for all \(300\) months, plus an additional annuity of \(\$800\) for the first \(180\) months.

steps.title: Select factors from the table
steps.bodyLatex: Using the column for \(r=0.002\), identify the present value interest factors: for \(n=300\), the factor is \(225.430\); for \(n=180\), the factor is \(151.036\).

steps.title: Calculate the total present value
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate the present value of both components: \(PV = 1200 \times 225.430 + 800 \times 151.036\). This simplifies to \(270516 + 120828.80 = 391344.80\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to convert the annual interest rate to a monthly rate.
Attempting to calculate the present value of the second annuity at month 181 without discounting it back to time zero (the decomposition method avoids this error).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ensure the total withdrawal amount is covered; the sum of the two annuities correctly accounts for the higher initial withdrawal rate.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This problem uses the product rule to find a derivative, then applies that result to evaluate an integral by manipulating the integrand.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that the derivative of \(x^2 an x\) involves terms that appear when expanding the integrand. The key is to use the identity \( an^2 x = \sec^2 x - 1\) to transform the integrand into a form that includes the derivative found in part (a).

steps.title: Differentiate the product
steps.bodyLatex: Apply the product rule \(\frac{d}{dx}(uv)=u\frac{dv}{dx}+v\frac{du}{dx}\) with \(u=x^2\) and \(v=\tan x\). Thus \(\frac{d}{dx}(x^2\tan x)=x^2\sec^2 x+2x\tan x\).

steps.title: Expand the integrand
